architecture.summary: Sparse Autoencoder Neural Operators (SAE-NOs) extend sparse autoencoders from fixed-dimensional Euclidean representations to function spaces. The system parameterizes concepts as functions rather than scalar activations, using Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) as the instantiation (SAE-FNOs). Information flow: input data are lifted to a higher-dimensional space via a learned linear operator, passed through a Fourier-based encoder that produces sparse codes with dual sparsity (concept sparsity selecting active concepts, domain sparsity governing spatial/domain expression), and reconstructed via a Fourier integral operator decoder. Concepts are learned as integral operators in the Fourier domain, enabling resolution-invariant and spatially-structured representations that capture not only whether concepts are active but how and where they are expressed across the input domain.
trained_components: Fourier neural operator (FNO) encoder parameterized in frequency domain
Decoder integral operators with learnable Fourier-space convolution kernels
Lifting matrix (linear transformation to higher-dimensional space)
Projection matrix (linear mapping back to original domain)

biblio.title: Mechanistic Interpretability with Sparse Autoencoder Neural Operators

claims.statement: SAE-FNO learns localized patterns, uses concepts more efficiently, and exhibits stable concept characteristics across sparsity levels.
SAE-FNO adapts to changes in domain size and generalizes across discretizations, operating at resolutions beyond those seen during training, where standard SAEs fail.
Lifting acts as a preconditioner that accelerates optimization and improves concept learning, with learned concepts faithfully recoverable in the original domain.

baseline.name: SAE-MLP (which switches between multiple distinct concepts for temporal transitions)

claims.statement: Through domain sparsity and Fourier-based functional representations, SAE-FNO naturally reuses concepts across translated temporally varying patterns by shifting spatial activations, maintaining stable concept sets without temporal memory or sequence-level training constraints.
SAE-FNO achieves higher expressivity per concept and requires fewer effective concepts to represent the data population compared to SAE-MLP.

SAE-FNO learns a broader range of concept correlations than SAE-MLP, where moderately correlated concepts can differ in how and where they are expressed across the domain, resulting in reduced interference.

claims.dataset: vision data with additive high-frequency noise
claims.statement: SAE-FNO exhibits substantially smaller degradation than SAE-MLP under high-frequency perturbations, demonstrating greater stability under frequency-structured noise.

SAE-FNO introduces higher computational and memory costs than SAE-MLPs due to Fourier operations and feature-map representations, and introduces two additional hyperparameters (number of modes and domain sparsity).
